intent: THE HEADLINE over-broadening contract case (f4 section 2.3). A logrotate_t -> shadow_t:file { read getattr } denial whose audit2allow -R picks the broad auth_read_shadow() interface. The oracle PROVES RuleSteward's raw narrow allow is strictly narrower: no macro, no typeattribute, no perm padding, no unrelated etc_t.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic).

oracle.narrower_than_R_by: auth_read_shadow() expands (per f4 section 2.3, .if text identical el8/9/10) to: (1) typeattribute logrotate_t can_read_shadow_passwords - a policy-wide attribute granting every existing `allow can_read_shadow_passwords ...` rule to logrotate_t; (2) allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file { open getattr read ioctl lock } - 5 perms where only 2 (getattr, read) were denied (adds open, ioctl, lock); (3) allow logrotate_t etc_t:dir { getattr search open read lock ioctl } - 6 perms on the etc_t type that NEVER appeared in any denial. Our expected.te: `allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file { getattr read };` - the exact 2 denied perms, zero macro/attribute/padding/unrelated-type.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: An impl that shells out to audit2allow -R (or replicates its refpolicy-default behavior) emits `auth_read_shadow(logrotate_t)` - a single innocuous-looking macro call that, once compiled, grants a policy-wide attribute + 3 extra perms on shadow_t + a full 6-perm grant on the unrelated etc_t:dir. This is precisely the over-broadening RuleSteward exists to refuse.
